model_narrative: We must purchase a required total number of units from a group of vendors, each of whom has submitted a bid in the form of a piecewise-linear schedule that relates the quantity bought to its total cost. For each vendor we decide an operating point along that vendor's schedule by choosing interpolation weights on the vendor's breakpoints, which determines how much is bought from that vendor and at what cost. The objective is to minimize the total purchase cost across all vendors.

sets.name: v
sets.doc: the set of vendors who have submitted bids

sets.name: s
sets.doc: the ordered set of bid segments; each vendor offers a piecewise-linear price-quantity schedule and the segments are the ordered breakpoints of that schedule, with 'nodeal' representing the option of purchasing nothing from the vendor

sets.name: vs
sets.doc: the set of valid vendor-segment bid possibilities; only some segments are defined for each vendor, so this set lists exactly which (vendor, segment) breakpoints actually exist

params.name: req
params.doc: the total number of units that must be purchased across all vendors, a single scalar

params.name: qmax
params.doc: the quantity associated with each vendor-segment breakpoint, in units; the purchased quantity from a vendor is the interpolation of these breakpoint quantities using the purchase-level weights

params.name: cost
params.doc: the total cost associated with each vendor-segment breakpoint, in dollars; the cost incurred from a vendor is the interpolation of these breakpoint costs using the purchase-level weights

vars.name: pl
vars.doc: the purchase-level interpolation weight placed on each vendor-segment breakpoint; the weights on a vendor's breakpoints select an operating point along that vendor's piecewise-linear schedule

vars.name: c
vars.doc: the total purchase cost across all vendors, in dollars

constraints.name: req
constraints.intent: The total quantity purchased across all vendors must exactly meet the required amount. For every vendor-segment breakpoint, weight its quantity by the purchase level chosen for that breakpoint, and the sum of these weighted quantities over all breakpoints must equal the required total number of units.

constraints.name: c
constraints.intent: The total purchase cost is the cost obtained by interpolating along each vendor's schedule. For every vendor-segment breakpoint, weight its cost by the purchase level chosen for that breakpoint, and set the total cost equal to the sum of these weighted costs over all breakpoints.

constraints.name: pl[v,
constraints.intent: For each vendor, the purchase-level weights placed on that vendor's breakpoints must sum to one, so that the chosen operating point is a valid interpolation along the vendor's schedule.

query: The two suppliers sitting on the bench in this plan, B and D, are exactly the ones we want to keep warm for next year. There's a proposal on the table to make those two carry a real share of the order together — at least 80,000 units of volume between the pair, however the model wants to split it. What total purchase cost does the optimizer return once that floor is in place?
expected_pyomo: model.spread_bd = Constraint(expr=sum(model.qmax[vv, s] * model.pl[vv, s] for (vv, s) in model.vs if vv == 'b') + sum(model.qmax[vv, s] * model.pl[vv, s] for (vv, s) in model.vs if vv == 'd') >= 80000)

query: Vendor C is winning the lion's share of the volume right now, and the risk committee wants to dilute that concentration. We're looking at a single-source ceiling: no one vendor should account for more than 40 percent of the total units we end up buying across the whole field. Plug that in and tell me where the total purchase bill lands.
expected_pyomo: model.share_c = Constraint(expr=sum(model.qmax[vv, s] * model.pl[vv, s] for (vv, s) in model.vs if vv == 'c') <= 0.4 * sum(model.qmax[v, s] * model.pl[v, s] for (v, s) in model.vs))

query: Procurement is pitching a broad-base sourcing rule for resilience: every supplier in the field should be carrying a meaningful minimum of the order rather than some walking away with nothing. Say we set that floor at 10,000 units for each vendor. What does the optimizer return for the total purchase cost under that rule?

query: The board is weighing a two-part resilience package on this award: trim vendor C's volume down to no more than 120,000 units so we're not so reliant on them, and at the same time guarantee vendor D a real foothold of at least 10,000 units. We want to evaluate the pair together as one scenario — what total purchase cost does that combination produce?
expected_pyomo:
model.cap_c = Constraint(expr=sum(model.qmax[vv, s] * model.pl[vv, s] for (vv, s) in model.vs if vv == 'c') <= 120000)
model.floor_d = Constraint(expr=sum(model.qmax[vv, s] * model.pl[vv, s] for (vv, s) in model.vs if vv == 'd') >= 10000)

query: It bothers me that vendor D walks away with zero business while we lean so hard on vendor A. For supply security I'd expect D to be pulling at least as much volume as A is. Shouldn't the plan have D carrying no fewer units than vendor A? What's keeping the model from balancing those two the way I'd expect?
expected_pyomo: model.match_da = Constraint(expr=sum(model.qmax[vv, s] * model.pl[vv, s] for (vv, s) in model.vs if vv == 'd') >= sum(model.qmax[vv, s] * model.pl[vv, s] for (vv, s) in model.vs if vv == 'a'))

query: Almost the entire order is funneled through just two suppliers, A and C, and that concentration makes me nervous. Couldn't we just hold the combined volume from those two below 120,000 units and spread the rest around? What's the tradeoff the model is making by piling so much onto that pair?
expected_pyomo: model.cap_ac = Constraint(expr=sum(model.qmax[vv, s] * model.pl[vv, s] for (vv, s) in model.vs if vv == 'a') + sum(model.qmax[vv, s] * model.pl[vv, s] for (vv, s) in model.vs if vv == 'c') <= 120000)

query: The plan dumps a huge slug of volume on vendor C while the others stay light. From a concentration-risk standpoint I'd want a uniform ceiling — no single supplier carrying more than 120,000 units. Wouldn't it be obvious to cap every vendor at that level? What's the model seeing that makes leaving C so loaded the better call?

query: I'd like to dual-source against vendor C without blowing up the plan: give vendor D a real foothold of at least 6,000 units and at the same time trim C back to no more than 140,000. Shouldn't the model be willing to make that kind of de-risking move? Walk me through what's pulling it the other way when I tie those two together.
expected_pyomo:
model.floor_d2 = Constraint(expr=sum(model.qmax[vv, s] * model.pl[vv, s] for (vv, s) in model.vs if vv == 'd') >= 6000)
model.cap_c2 = Constraint(expr=sum(model.qmax[vv, s] * model.pl[vv, s] for (vv, s) in model.vs if vv == 'c') <= 140000)
